title: Pithecoctenium vitalba
intro: Pithecoctenium vitalba, also known as the "cat's claw vine," is a climbing plant native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, characterized by its unique hooked thorns and woody stems. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which can help to reduce inflammation, improve immune function, and protect against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Pithecoctenium vitalba include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, including the anti-inflammatory compounds vitexilactone and isovitexilactone, as well as the antioxidant compound epicatechin. Pithecoctenium vitalba is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, capsules, and tinctures, which are often used to support joint health, immune function, and overall well-being.
taxonomy: Pithecoctenium vitalba is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. This plant is native to Central and South America. It belongs to the genus Pithecoctenium, which consists of a few species of vines and climbing plants. The Apocynaceae family is a large and diverse group of plants that also includes milkweeds, dogbanes, and oleander. Pithecoctenium vitalba is often found in tropical and subtropical regions with high humidity and warm temperatures.

side_effects: Pithecoctenium vitalba, also known as the poisonwood vine, is considered toxic and can cause a range of side effects. These side effects include dermatitis, skin irritation, and allergic reactions upon contact with the plant's sap. Ingesting any part of the plant can lead to more severe side effects, such as gastrointestinal issues and potentially life-threatening allergic reactions.
